title: Using home compost in raised beds
subtitle: Mixing ratios, application depths, and what to expect from compost of varying maturity
excerpt: Home compost applied correctly to a raised bed improves soil structure, feeds the soil food web, and reduces the need for imported growing media. But compost that is too fresh pulls nitrogen from the soil as it finishes decomposing. This guide covers how to assess compost maturity, what mixing ratio to use, and how to apply it without damaging young plants.
sourceNotes: RHS guidance on using home compost in beds. HDRA (Garden Organic) composting and soil fertility guides. BSI PAS 100:2018 on compost quality specification.

glossaryTerms.definition: A managed composting process that maintains temperatures of 55 to 70 degrees Celsius through active turning and balanced carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, decomposing material in 4 to 12 weeks.
The carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of composting material. Browns (high carbon) and greens (high nitrogen) should be balanced at roughly 25:1 to 30:1 for active hot composting.
Liquid that drains from a compost heap or bin, rich in dissolved nutrients and microbial activity. Can be diluted 10:1 and used as a liquid feed.

content.text: A well-finished home compost is dark, crumbly, and smells of earth. Applied at 50 to 75 mm depth in autumn, it improves drainage in clay soil and water retention in sandy soil. It also feeds the worm and microbial population that drives nutrient cycling. The risk is applying compost too early: unfinished compost with a high
C:N ratio
temporarily locks up soil nitrogen as soil microbes finish the decomposition, which can cause yellowing and slow growth in spring-sown crops.
Assessing compost maturity
Colour: finished compost is very dark brown to black. Pale brown or greenish compost is unfinished.
Smell: finished compost smells earthy, not sour, not ammonia, not putrid. Any bad smell means insufficient decomposition.
Texture: finished compost crumbles easily. Fibrous or chunky material is not finished.
Temperature: a finished heap has a stable temperature close to ambient. A heap still composting actively will be 10 to 20 degrees Celsius warmer than the surrounding air.
Application rates and timing

For a standard raised bed (1.2 m wide by 2.4 m long, 30 cm deep), apply 50 to 75 mm of finished compost in autumn, after the final harvest. Do not dig it in: lay it on the surface and let worms and frost action incorporate it over winter. By spring, the surface layer has partly disappeared into the soil. For spring application, reduce to 25 to 30 mm and apply at least four weeks before sowing to allow the compost to settle and soil temperature to recover.
Using partially matured compost

Compost that is partially matured can be used as a mulch around established perennials and shrubs. It will continue decomposing on the surface without direct root contact. A partially matured heap is still warm with some fibrous material present. Do not use partially matured compost around seedlings or in seed-sowing mixes: the nitrogen drawdown will stunt germination.
Hot composting

kills weed seeds at sustained temperatures above 55 degrees Celsius; cold composting does not. Compost from a cold heap will contain viable weed seeds and should be sieved and applied as a mulch rather than incorporated into growing media.
Sieving and use in containers

Sieve finished compost through a 10 mm mesh to remove large woody fragments. The fine fraction is suitable for mixing with topsoil at a 1:3 ratio (one part compost, three parts topsoil) for raised bed establishment or container growing. Do not use home compost at 100 percent in containers: it compacts easily and has variable nutrient content. The rejected coarse fraction from sieving goes back into the compost heap as a carbon amendment. The
leachate
draining from the compost bin can be collected, diluted 10:1 with water, and used as a liquid feed.

The compost was not fully finished. The decomposing organic matter is temporarily locking up soil nitrogen. Apply a liquid nitrogen feed (diluted nettle or seaweed feed) and wait for the compost to finish decomposing over 4 to 6 weeks.

attrs.symptom: Compost surface growing white mould after application

content.text: Fungal mycelium is colonising the surface compost layer and continuing decomposition. This is harmless and beneficial. It should disappear as soil microbes and worms break it down within a few weeks.
